S.t: Mapping literature with ResearchRabbit, then applying the CRAAP test
S.s: Students map related literature from a seed paper using ResearchRabbit, then critically evaluate sources with the CRAAP test (currency, relevance, accuracy, authors, purpose).

S.t: Building on (or ignoring) a ChatGPT essay answer
S.s: Students receive a ChatGPT essay response on prenuptial agreements and must demonstrably 'add value that an AI cannot' when using, building on, or ignoring it.

S.t: Classroom evaluation of a ChatGPT summary, then redesigned exam questions
S.inst: Dept of Geography and Earth Sciences
S.s: Students critique a ChatGPT summary of 'flashbulb memory' for bias and limitations in class, then answer exam questions redesigned to be problem-based and applied.

S.t: Using GAI as a research tool in group marketing-strategy assessments, then reflecting on its role
S.by: Lucill Curtis, Associate Professor in Marketing
S.inst: University of East Anglia, Norwich Business School
S.s: On an MBA 'Marketing and Customer Focus' module (35 students), two group formative tasks had students use GAI as a research tool to identify market segments and build campaign plans for a company, before an individual summative task asked them to design and justify their own marketing strategy.

S.t: The '4Rs' method: rewriting exam questions until ChatGPT can no longer answer them
S.s: Each online maths-for-social-science test question was run through ChatGPT-3.5 in a Reflect-Rewrite-Retest-Review cycle; any question it could answer correctly was rewritten to demand higher-order thinking (per Bloom's Taxonomy) and retested, repeating until AI-resistant — 66 original questions were reviewed and turned into 89 redesigned ones across three tests.

S.t: Generate-summarise-reflect: a staff CPD exercise using an AI-written cannabis-legalisation essay
S.s: In a staff CPD workshop, academics used AI to generate a 500-word essay on 'Should cannabis be legalised?', summarised it in their own words within a 100-word limit, then wrote a 500-word reflection on the skills the exercise developed — modelling an authentic-assessment exercise staff could adapt for students.

S.t: A two-part AI-literacy assessment: an AI-output portfolio, then a live 'Professional Discussion'
S.by: Liam Newton, Senior Lecturer in Business and Management
S.s: A new AI-literacy module has BA Business students produce a portfolio of three distinct AI-generated business outputs (each from a different AI tool) with reflections and process logs, then defend their critical understanding in a 30-minute in-person 'Professional Discussion' on an unseen AI case study.
S.q: It's honestly one of the most valuable things I've studied at university... I think this module had the right balance of theory and practice. (Student, 2024-25 cohort)

S.t: 'Liam Chat': a custom AI generating personalised, deliberately flawed SQL scripts for each student
S.by: Prof. Rania Shibl and Dr Judy Watson
S.inst: Southern Cross University & University of the Sunshine Coast
S.s: A custom tool built on OpenAI's API, 'Liam Chat', generates a unique flawed SQL schema and dataset for each student in a 200-student database design course; students must diagnose and correct the errors themselves rather than just produce a solution.

S.t: Critiquing a deliberately flawed Copilot essay, under exam conditions
S.s: For a final-year Physiological Disorders exam, the lecturer used Microsoft Copilot to generate an 800-word essay on diabetic retinopathy, deliberately inserted one obvious and one subtle factual error, then had students critique it in 90 minutes under exam conditions — replacing the traditional blank-page essay exam.

S.t: Splitting a literature review into essay + annotated bibliography + reflective table to make research reasoning visible
S.by: Denise Hough, Victoria Paterson, and Prof. Barbara K Mable
S.s: A final-year literature review (around 100 students/year) was restructured into three parts — an 80%-weighted essay, an ungraded annotated bibliography of the top 20 search results, and a 20%-weighted reflective table — with explicit stage-by-stage GenAI guidance embedded rather than a blanket ban or requirement.
S.q: It was the first time in my degree getting specific advice on the appropriate use of GenAI in scientific writing, finding it refreshing and valuable to have information clearly laid out. (Final year student)

S.t: Enhancing Design Thinking in Education
S.s: Applies generative AI within a design-thinking teaching framework.

S.t: The use of GenAI in teaching arts management
S.s: GenAI integrated into an arts management curriculum.

S.t: Student-Staff Co-Creation of Politics of Artificial Intelligence
S.s: Students and staff co-created course content examining the politics of AI.

S.t: Openly using AI in a French Hotel Management School
S.s: Transparent, sanctioned AI use within a hospitality-management programme.

S.t: AI Client Simulation for Counselling Skills Development
S.by: Beverley Pickard-Jones, Luke Sanders, Fay Short
S.inst: Bangor University, School of Psychology and Sport Science
S.s: Trainee counsellors run simulated therapy sessions with ChatGPT 3.5 prompted to embody clients with varied presentations and personalities, then request AI performance feedback.

S.t: Use of GenAI in healthcare education to enhance representation
S.s: Uses GenAI imagery to diversify representation in healthcare teaching materials.

S.t: Above board: explicit use of ChatGPT and other online tools
S.s: A transparency-first case study on openly sanctioned ChatGPT use in coursework.

S.t: Integrating ChatGPT in Authentic Peer Marking Assessment
S.s: Student groups design chemical process plants with ChatGPT at each stage, then peer-evaluate each other's AI-assisted designs across groups; over 80% reported the integration positively.

S.t: Turning written feedback into an AI-generated poem
S.inst: School of Education, University of Leeds
S.s: Fed real summative-assignment feedback (577 words) into ChatGPT to generate a feedback poem, and trialled it with one student, who found the experiment useful but flagged concerns about using it on a failing piece of work.

S.t: Comparing ChatGPT-3's moral and critical reasoning across two contexts
S.inst: School of Languages, Cultures and Societies, University of Leeds
S.s: Put the same real oral-history interview extract (a Spanish Civil War child refugee) to ChatGPT-3 in two threads with differing context, then compared the scientific, educational and moral quality of its answers, publishing the full dialogues on a personal blog.

S.t: A working AI persona simulator for research-interview practice
S.inst: Werklund School of Education, University of Calgary
S.s: Built and deployed a live web tool using the OpenAI API that impersonates a user-defined persona so student-researchers can practice interview technique and generate authentic-feeling qualitative data without ethics review.

S.t: A working GPT-3 rubric generator for educators
S.inst: Werklund School of Education, University of Calgary
S.s: Built and deployed a live GPT-3 tool that generates a draft marking rubric from a given learning outcome, including adjustments for non-native English users, as a starting point for educators to customise.

S.t: Drafting most of a module's activities with ChatGPT
S.s: Used ChatGPT prompts to draft most of a module's student activities (writer's purpose, blurbs, topic sentences, thesis statements) and feedback comments, editing the output before publication in a coursebook.

S.t: Generating phonetic-script practice questions with ChatGPT
S.s: Discovered ChatGPT could produce International Phonetic Alphabet script and used it to build a 10-item multiple-choice vowel-sound identification activity.

S.t: Using ChatGPT as a translation buddy for an open educational game
S.inst: School of Education, University of Leeds
S.s: Used ChatGPT to produce a first-draft Greek translation of instructions for an open educational game under development, then critically revised the output as a qualified translator.

S.t: Self-assessment against ChatGPT's own exam answers
S.s: Formative assessment activity where students solve an exam-style question, compare it against ChatGPT's answer and feedback, then critique and mark both their own and ChatGPT's responses.

S.t: Writing with ChatGPT and submitting the edits as evidence of critical thinking
S.s: In a compulsory core business course with mainly international students, has students write with ChatGPT and then submit their edits, requiring them to demonstrate deep thinking and fact-checked, linked practice.

S.t: Testing ChatGPT for extracting UML conceptual classes
S.s: Tested ChatGPT's ability to extract a list of candidate conceptual classes from a natural-language specification, to give Software Engineering students a critically-assessed first draft for their UML class diagrams.

S.t: A fully AI-generated podcast, script to audio
S.s: Produced a podcast entirely generated by AI — script by ChatGPT, cover art by DALL-E 2, narration by Speechify, mastered in iZotope oZone — documented in a companion 'how we did it' blog post.

S.t: AI-generated absurd-premise texts to teach genre conventions
S.inst: Language Centre, University of the Arts London
S.s: Used the OpenAI playground/ChatGPT to generate deliberately absurd texts (a job application for 'the King's toothpaste squeezer', an obituary for a toothbrush) so students could identify genre conventions before writing their own.

S.t: A Foundation Year exercise comparing ChatGPT output to real academic profiles
S.s: Foundation Year Psychology students research a real academic from their own department via ChatGPT and compare its output against the academic's actual staff profile page, revising prompts to test accuracy.

S.t: Ongoing classroom experiments applying ChatGPT to sentence-level style
S.s: Extended existing sentence-imitation and sentence-combining exercises by having students and instructor experiment together with ChatGPT to explore syntactic fluency and stylistic choice at the sentence level.

S.t: Testing ChatGPT's consistency on the same essay question, reworded
S.s: Ran a real post-structuralism essay title through ChatGPT with slight rewordings to test how consistent and well-sourced its answers were, designing a fact-checking exercise for students to replicate on their own assessment titles.

S.t: Rehearsing real fieldwork interview questions on a ChatGPT-roleplayed flood warden
S.s: Second-year Natural Hazard Management students converse with ChatGPT prompted to role-play an experienced, previously-flooded community flood warden, to refine their interview questions before interviewing a real warden.

S.t: A weekly prompt-and-response query log with generative AI
S.s: Students maintain a weekly log recording their generative-AI questions and answers, varying prompting strategies week to week, using a shared template to practice and observe iterative prompting.

S.t: A course where students use AI to draft institutional AI-usage policy
S.s: Facilitated a full course where students used ChatGPT and other tools to research and draft real institutional AI-usage policy recommendations, publishing syllabi and a first draft of the students' usage policy online.

S.t: Students blog on ChatGPT explanations of philosophical concepts
S.s: MPhil Digital Curation students used ChatGPT to source explanations of two philosophical concepts, then blogged on their affective experience of the tool, their resulting understanding, and the appropriateness of the AI-generated text for academic discourse, followed by a live discussion.

S.t: DALL-E emotion cards for a Dixit-style peer feedback game
S.s: Students describe an emotion and its context, turn the description into a DALL-E prompt, and select the generated image that best captures it, then use the resulting cards to play a Dixit-style emotion-guessing board game.

S.t: Marking AI-generated courseworks against real assessment criteria
S.inst: Greater Manchester Business School, University of Bolton
S.s: Used in an HE6 module: generated AI-written courseworks from real assessment prompts using ChatGPT, Copilot and Bard, then had students mark them against the module's actual criteria to expose gaps in technical language and critical analysis.

S.t: A song written and translated by ChatGPT, tested against a real dialect
S.s: The Dean of Science wrote a song with ChatGPT and had it translate the lyrics into his own local dialect, testing — and being surprised by — the translation quality.

S.t: AI-generated art as a mediator for peer feedback
S.by: Paola Corti, Daniela Casiraghi and Bianca Santolini
S.s: Peer reviewers write text feedback against a negotiated rubric, then use ChatGPT to craft a DALL-E prompt and generate an accompanying image, publishing the method as an open Art-based Feedback Toolkit.

S.t: A research assignment using ChatGPT and elicit.org alongside academic sources
S.s: Final-course assignment where learning-analytics students write a 3-page article on AI's impact on their field using ChatGPT and elicit.org together with 3 academic and 2 popular articles as required sources.

S.t: Recreando el pasado con IA: generating images of daily life in the Porfiriato
S.inst: Facultad de Filosofía y Letras, UNAM, Mexico
S.s: Pedagogy undergraduates generated images of everyday life and education under Mexico's Porfiriato using DALL-E 2, Stability AI and Deep AI, then posted them with titles and descriptions to a class Padlet for a History of Education course.

S.t: Generating tailored counselling case studies for practice discussion
S.s: Used ChatGPT to generate novel clinical case studies tailored to the specific ethical and clinical topics counselling students were currently finding difficult, for small-group discussion practice.

S.t: Redesigning a mock job application so each student gets an AI-generated unique role
S.s: Redesigned a mock counselling job-application assessment, at students' own suggestion, so each student uses ChatGPT to generate their own unique job description to apply for, replacing a shared 'describe and evaluate' essay.

S.t: Peer-marking ChatGPT's own generated answers before marking each other
S.s: Students generate a range of ChatGPT responses to a real assessment question, then synthesise, critique and mark them against set criteria — building confidence before peer-assessing each other's work.

S.t: Feeding a student's full memoir essay into DALL-E to generate illustrations
S.s: When students wondered how to illustrate their multimodal memoirs, the instructor plugged an entire student essay into DALL-E as a prompt and worked with the class to iteratively refine it into more relevant images.

S.t: Demonstrating gender bias in DALL-E's 'Robotics Engineer' images
S.s: Generated DALL-E images for the prompt 'Robotics Engineer', found them predominantly male, and used the result to teach engineering faculty and students about implicit bias reflected in AI training data.

S.t: Comparing a real ChatGPT critical assessment against a human-written one
S.s: Students annotate orientation videos on ChatGPT, then read a real NYT article alongside a sample ChatGPT critical assessment and a human-written one, published as an activity via Canvas Commons.

S.t: Critiquing a ChatGPT answer against academic literature in Project Management
S.s: Students pose a Project Management question to ChatGPT, then write a mini-essay giving both positive and negative academically-referenced criticism of its answer, including its hallucinations.

S.t: Testing ChatGPT's ability to generate code for real programming assessments
S.s: PhD researcher tested ChatGPT's ability to translate real programming-assessment specifications into working code segments, assessing how well its output met the assessment prompt.

S.t: Piloting ChatGPT for co-creating target-language content
S.s: Currently piloting having language students use ChatGPT to help generate content in the target language, extending existing student co-creation work built around MS Forms quizzes and ThingLink mind maps.

S.t: Playing Google's QuickDraw and Autodraw to surface AI training bias
S.s: Played Google's QuickDraw (which guesses a doodle in progress) and Autodraw, and documented on a blog how the training data's cultural assumptions surfaced — e.g. failing to recognise an Arabic-labelled doodle of an angel.

S.t: A proof-of-concept branching nursing scenario authored end-to-end by AI
S.s: Built a proof-of-concept branching therapeutic-communication nursing scenario with all dialogue and feedback generated by ChatGPT and imagery by Midjourney, flagged as requiring subject-matter-expert vetting before use, as part of a federally funded $2.5M OER nursing project.

S.t: Writing and self-publishing a book using ChatGPT and DALL-E
S.s: Wrote and self-published a book, retaining copyright, using ChatGPT for text and DALL-E for illustrations, distributed via the Google Play Books Partner Center.

S.t: Iteratively refining a DALL-E2 image to match a written concept
S.s: Students select a term, describe it in words fed to DALL-E2, write a five-minute reflective essay on the resulting image, then adapt their prompt wording to produce an improved image reflecting their understanding.

S.t: From exemplars to GAI: activating assessment literacy
S.by: Dr Helen Thompson, Dr Tim Davies
S.s: Students mark anonymized exemplars, compare against real grades on Padlet, then use GenAI to analyze assessment-criteria language and simulate peer feedback. Fewer 2:2 grades and higher confidence followed over two years.
S.q: Anonymised surveys indicate increased confidence and preparedness.

S.t: Integrating an AI Chatbot for enhanced student learning in a laboratory module
S.s: Video case study on embedding a chatbot within physics laboratory teaching.

S.t: Exploring the impact of Generative AI on Modern Language Learning
S.s: Language students across Arabic, Chinese, French, German, Italian, Japanese, Russian and Spanish completed a writing task both with and without ChatGPT, then took part in reflective interviews and focus groups on the experience. Findings on the benefits and risks students identified fed directly into the School's new GenAI policy.

S.t: Using ChatGPT 4.0 to create essay plans
S.s: A single detailed prompt on the Great Irish Famine produced a ready-to-use essay structure students found reassuring and easy to follow, needing little refinement.

S.t: A 5-minute AI image activity using Microsoft Copilot
S.s: A quick, structured in-class activity: students prompt Bing/Copilot for a themed AI image, then share and discuss results as a light introduction to AI tools.

S.t: Creating multiple-choice quizzes with ChatGPT + Microsoft Forms
S.s: A single prompt to ChatGPT 3.5 generates a 10-question self-marking quiz on plumbing health & safety, imported straight into Microsoft Forms for instant student feedback and staff analytics.

S.t: Building an emergency cover lesson plan overnight with ChatGPT
S.s: With a colleague off sick and no cover, a scheme-of-learning export was fed into ChatGPT 3.5 to produce two full 2-hour lesson plans with activities and quizzes in 30 minutes.
S.q: [ChatGPT] changed the way I work for the better.

S.t: An 'Immunology Wars' mega game built with ChatGPT4 and DALL-E 3
S.s: Multi-session prompting built a Star Wars-themed trading game where student groups collect immune components to fight infections; DALL-E 3 generated the imagery.

S.t: Educational podcasts using a cloned voice and ChatGPT-written scripts
S.s: ElevenLabs clones the lecturer's own voice; ChatGPT4 writes revision-podcast scripts from a fixed template, cutting a 5-minute podcast's production time from 2 hours to about 10 minutes.

S.t: A scalable approach to creating 130+ Custom GPTs for vocational marking
S.s: 130+ Custom GPTs, each aligned to specific units and awarding-body standards, generate feedback and support moderation; learners get 48-hour access to a self-assessment GPT before final submission. Est. 10–15 hrs/week saved per staff member.

S.t: Feedback Tutor in Your Pocket
S.s: Custom GPTs configured with module rubrics give students rubric-aligned draft feedback before submission, across Law, Computing, and Health disciplines. In Therapeutic Radiography, average grade rose 51%→54% and fail rate fell 20%→17%.
S.q: It helped me understand what I needed to improve, like having a tutor on call 24/7.

S.t: Generating ideas and images in MA Advertising and Design
S.s: Students use GenAI for ideation (morphological matrix technique) and may submit AI-generated images, since image-craft skill isn't the module's focus; polished AI images didn't always communicate ideas better.

S.t: Critical comparisons: students vs ChatGPT
S.s: Students solve an exam question alone, then compare against and critique ChatGPT's answer and feedback, before marking both and discussing in class.

S.t: Critical thinking in public history
S.s: Instead of a standard essay prompt, the instructor feeds the question to ChatGPT for a short answer; students' 2,000-word essay is their agreement or disagreement with it.

S.t: Abstracting 500 pages of 20th-century wasp taxonomy into a spreadsheet
S.s: A single detailed prompt abstracted 500 pages of scientific papers into a 10-column spreadsheet; a 50-row accuracy check found just two minor errors.
S.q: Don't be afraid of long and quite complex prompts — the more detail, the better the AI performs.

S.t: ChatGPT as both admin assistant and 'critical friend' reviewer
S.by: Sara Ratner, PI, AIEOU Research Hub
S.s: Uses ChatGPT to draft newsletters and reshape research for policymakers, and separately assigns it a role-prompted 'critical reviewer' to flag weaknesses in her own drafts.
S.q: I ask myself what don't I want to do — and can AI do it for me?

S.t: Socratic revision partner during the Astrophoria Foundation Year
S.s: Students used ChatGPT Edu's voice mode, image upload, and Projects for Socratic step-by-step tutoring and note summarizing during revision.

S.t: Replacing radioactive contrast injections with a GenAI 'digital tracer'
S.by: Regent Lee, Professor of Interdisciplinary Innovation
S.s: Patented a deep-learning method synthesizing 'digital contrast' that removes the need for radiopharmaceutical injections in CT/PET cancer imaging.

S.t: Drafting grant sections and turning transcripts into formatted minutes
S.s: Uses ChatGPT Projects as a persistent reference base to draft grant sections and turn permitted meeting transcripts into minutes with action owners.

S.t: A custom GPT trained on the division's own finance regulations
S.s: Built a custom GPT trained on university financial regulations so colleagues can self-serve reimbursement questions instead of asking staff directly.
S.q: Use [ChatGPT] as a cognitive enhancement tool, do not outsource your brain.

S.t: A custom GPT primed with exact server/OS/tooling context
S.s: A custom GPT pre-loaded with his OS/server/institutional context, integrated with VS Code, cut bug-fixing time by roughly a third.

S.t: A custom GPT that mines a work diary for underused skills
S.s: A custom GPT ingests her daily work diary to surface hard/soft skills demonstrated each week against her job description.

S.t: AI-assisted Annotations for Histology
S.by: Sharmila Saran Rajendran, Helen Christian, Mary McMenamin, Damion Young, Rumyana Smilevska
S.s: Grant-funded project on AI-assisted annotation of histology practicals.

S.t: AI-assisted Simulated Patient for Clinical Communication Skills Training
S.by: Sofia I R Pereira, Philip Drennan, Damion Young, et al.
S.s: A simulated-patient chatbot lets medical students practice clinical communication and reasoning.

S.t: AI-driven Feedback Loops for Student Evaluation
S.by: Judy Irving, Daniel Long, Kate Forrester, Matthew Hurst
S.s: Explores AI-driven feedback loops for evaluating student progress.

S.t: AI for Learning: Foundation Year Students Using ChatGPT Effectively
S.by: Christopher Patrick, Rachel Quarrell, Nicole Miranda
S.s: Helps Physical Sciences Foundation Year students learn to use ChatGPT effectively for study.

S.t: AIID (Artificial Intelligence for Interdisciplinary Discussion)
S.by: Jeremy Knox, Rebecca Eynon, Lulu Shi
S.s: Uses AI to match and structure interdisciplinary discussion among students.

S.t: Brainard the Fox (Interactive Bibliography Tool)
S.by: Juliana Dresvina, Daniel Gerrard, Leif Dixon, Gavin Thomas
S.s: An interactive AI bibliography tool built for humanities research and teaching.

S.t: Compsci Course Guide Chatbot
S.by: Jennifer Watson, Edward Crichton, Lucy Sajdler
S.s: A chatbot that guides computer science students through course structure and options.

S.t: pAIpercast: Paper to Podcast Project
S.by: Stephen Taylor, Delia O'Rourke, Bana Alamad
S.s: Converts academic papers into podcast-style audio using generative AI.

S.t: Support for Neurodiverse Humanities Students
S.by: Simon Park, Machilu van Bever Donker, Phillip Rothwell, Siân Grønlie
S.s: Explores GenAI tools to support neurodiverse humanities students.

S.t: Teaching Research Reproducibility Through AI-Human Collaboration
S.by: Lucy Bowes, Nicholas Yeung, Laurence Hunt, Juuso Repo
S.s: Uses AI-human collaboration to teach the principles of reproducible research.

S.t: Voice Chatbot for Language Learning
S.by: Marion Sadoux, Cornelia Wiedenhofer, Jieun Kiaer, Emine Cakir
S.s: A voice-based AI chatbot for practicing spoken language learning.

S.t: Writing and Translation Feedback Tool
S.by: Claudia Kaiser, Cornelia Wiedenhofer, Nadine Buchmann
S.s: An AI feedback tool built for writing and translation coursework.

S.t: Addressing Institutional Generative AI Challenges through a Systems Approach
S.by: Prof. Gunter Saunders, Director of Digital Capability Development and AI Leadership
S.s: Piloted 'LIDE', an internal chatbot trained on Westminster's own learning-technology content to triage routine student and colleague queries, as part of a wider institutional response — governed by a public 'Westminster Position on Generative AI' setting out ethical, transparent use.

S.t: Using AI to Support Undergraduate Game Programming
S.by: Dr Darrel Greenhill, Associate Professor, School of Computer Science and Mathematics
S.s: Deploys AI to support discipline-specific coding instruction in game programming.

S.t: Embedding AI Literacy through a School-Wide, Nuanced Approach
S.s: In SOCET, one module leader required students to submit their Copilot prompt logs alongside assignments as a reflective record of AI use; another permitted AI tools for asset creation provided the core project work remained the student's own.

S.t: Using Generative AI to Help Productivity and Wellbeing of Professional Services Staff
S.by: Learning Innovation and Digital Engagement team
S.s: Supports administrative staff productivity and wellbeing via AI tools.

S.t: Trialling and Experimentation of AI Technologies to Support L&T&A
S.by: Wayne Leung and Tim Linsey, Learning and Teaching Enhancement Centre
S.s: Ran ongoing formal trials of chatbot tools, AI-assisted marking systems and AI research-assistants — including supplier-run demo sessions on campus and staff/student feedback surveys — to keep pace with fast-moving tools such as Google's NotebookLM without over-relying on any single AI environment.

S.t: Implementing AI Turnitin Detection
S.by: Steering group led by the Vice Principal Academic Development and Student Experience
S.s: Deploys Turnitin's AI-detection tooling with accompanying staff support.

S.t: Using AI to Support Students' Understanding of Statistical Applications
S.by: Dr Owen Spendiff, Head of Dept of Applied & Human Sciences
S.s: Applies AI tools to research-methods and statistics instruction.

S.t: Fostering Critical Thinking and Creativity in Spatial Design through AI Image Generation
S.by: Dr Sarah Stevens, Dr Marcus Winter, and Edward Crump
S.s: Integrates AI image-generation tools into spatial-design pedagogy.

S.t: Bringing AI into Creative Courses
S.by: Industry, University QA, and course Showrunner lead
S.s: Embeds AI tools within creative-discipline curricula.

S.t: Using AI to Build Confidence in Language Teaching
S.by: MA TESOL practical teaching module lecturer
S.s: Deploys AI to support and build confidence among language learners.

S.t: How AI is Supporting Patient Communication Assessments
S.inst: University of the West of England, Bristol
S.s: Uses AI to support healthcare students practicing patient-communication assessments.

S.t: Using Co-Pilot to Teach Critical Reasoning in Social Sciences
S.inst: University of the West of England, Bristol
S.s: Employs Microsoft Copilot to develop critical-reasoning skills in social science courses.

S.t: Student 'Pop-Ups': AI@KU Be Critical, Be Transparent
S.s: Ran seven campus pop-up events handing out AI-illustrated stickers and a spot-the-AI-manipulation photo quiz (over 1,200 student completions) to build critical, transparent engagement with generative AI, alongside live demos of Kingston's licensed AI tools.

S.t: Patient Medication Counselling Coach
S.s: A Cogniti-built AI coach generates varied simulated patient scenarios for dispensing practice, giving different responses each time and feedback aligned to Pharmacy Council case discussions.

S.t: Pharmacy Management Finance Tutor
S.s: A Cogniti agent embedded in Canvas gives Socratic responses on difficult financial topics; unit survey response rates more than doubled after launch.

S.t: Dr MattTabolism — an AI assistant for biochemistry
S.by: Matthew Clemson, Minh Huynh, Alice Huang
S.inst: School of Life and Environmental Sciences (BCMB2001)
S.s: A Cogniti assistant configured as a biochemistry expert, linked to lecture resources in Canvas, used Socratic dialogue; over 180 of 800 students engaged with it for revision.
S.q: This is the most innovative and creative piece of technology I have engaged with.

S.t: OT intervention-planning chatbot workshop
S.s: Customized Cogniti chatbots role-play a child with handwriting difficulties and his teacher, so OT students practice building and presenting intervention plans with realistic pushback.

S.t: Live ChatGPT demo on law and technology questions
S.s: Live in-class demonstration of ChatGPT answering law & technology questions, followed by small-group discussion recorded in a shared collaborative document.

S.t: Exposing ChatGPT's failures on exam questions
S.s: Demonstrated ChatGPT's limitations on exam questions about carbon sequestration; student groups then critiqued and marked up the errors in a shared document.

S.t: Generate, critique, and re-prompt on familiar topics
S.s: Students generate AI responses on topics they already know well, then critique the output's quality and collaboratively improve the prompt.

S.t: Trialling ChatGPT in French for research tasks
S.inst: Faculty of Arts and Social Sciences
S.s: Students trial ChatGPT in French for research tasks, then see its failures demonstrated live on literary-analysis questions.

S.t: ChatGPT as pre-class discussion kickstarter in finance units
S.s: Uses ChatGPT in pre-class exercises to seed discussion in postgraduate finance units.
S.q: My recommendation to students is to use ChatGPT for what it is: as a brainstorming buddy.

S.t: Contextualizing quantitative study design with ChatGPT examples
S.s: Students request field-specific examples from ChatGPT to make quantitative study-design concepts concrete, then the class evaluates the outputs together.

S.t: An AI double for Fundamentals of Chemistry
S.s: A Cogniti agent keeps answers succinct, checks comprehension with questions, and is deliberately programmed to avoid quiz answers and admin topics.

S.t: An AI double for group-work facilitation and OSCE roleplay
S.s: One Cogniti agent facilitates group projects and mediates discussion; another roleplays clinical exam scenarios with real-time feedback on communication and reasoning.

S.t: An AI double for synthesis-task writing feedback
S.s: A Cogniti agent checks argument coherence and paraphrasing quality in practice assessment writing against the marking rubric.

S.t: An AI double for OT goal-setting supervision
S.s: Acts as a clinical supervisor, guiding students to construct their own SMART goals through questioning rather than writing the goals for them.
S.q: It will help them to be real and authentic with their goal setting statements.

S.t: An AI double as a classroom simulator for emerging academics
S.s: Poses realistic, discipline-specific classroom scenarios requiring quick thinking, then gives constructive feedback on new tutors' responses.

S.t: 'Study Buddy' — an AI double for self-regulated learning
S.s: An evidence-based learning-theory coach helps students set goals, develop strategy, and monitor progress toward becoming self-regulated learners.

S.t: An AI double as a UN Sustainable Development Goals tutor
S.s: Runs Socratic conversations helping students contextualize the UN SDGs for company strategy, aimed at building a responsible business mindset.

S.t: An AI double for ANZCA pharmacology exam prep
S.s: Gives trainees 24/7 access to exam-style pharmacology questions spanning Bloom's taxonomy, with hints on incomplete answers.

S.t: An AI double for Socratic quantitative genetics tutoring
S.s: One-question-at-a-time Socratic dialogue built from lecture notes; chat logs help the teaching team see exactly where students get stuck.

S.t: 'Molecules on the Menu' administrative helper
S.s: Answers routine student queries on unit organization, assessments, and deadlines from Canvas resources, aimed at faster response in an asynchronous course.

S.t: An AI double for reliability-statistics Socratic tutoring
S.s: Teaches psychometric properties using a Socratic technique built around rubrics and resources, guiding exploration rather than handing over answers.

S.t: Using Generative AI for Legal Tutorial Assignments
S.s: Explores how GenAI could support legal-education tutorial assignment design.

S.t: GenAI for Improving Experimental Design in Cognitive Psychology
S.s: Investigates AI assistance for helping students develop better research methodologies.

S.t: Human-AI Collaboration for Inclusive Science Communication
S.s: Examines partnering with AI tools to make scientific content more accessible.

S.t: Generative AI and Hybrid Intelligence for Fostering Teacher Noticing
S.s: Develops hybrid AI systems supporting educators' ability to notice and respond to student learning patterns.

S.t: Generative AI and the Usefulness of ChatGPT in a Mathematics Learning Activity
S.s: Tests LLM integration within a quantitative mathematics course activity.

S.t: GenAI and Constructive Alignment in STEM
S.s: Applies hybrid intelligence to keep STEM course objectives, instruction, and assessment coherent.

S.t: Generative AI and Basic Psychological Needs of BA Students
S.s: Investigates how AI tools affect student autonomy, competence, and relatedness.

S.t: Student-AI Interactions Pilot Study, Co-Created with First-Year Science Students
S.s: Participatory research with novice learners on optimal AI-integration approaches.

S.t: GenAI-Generated Exemplars to Improve Student Scientific Writing
S.s: Uses AI-generated model exemplars to scaffold academic scientific-writing skills.

S.t: Effect of 'AI Tasks' on Student AI Literacy in a Fact-Check Assignment
S.s: Evaluates whether AI-integrated verification tasks improve student information literacy.

S.t: Generative AI and Assessment of AE
S.s: Addresses validity concerns when GenAI can produce assessment-relevant outputs.

S.t: GenAI and Writing Assignments in Legal Education
S.s: Redesigns law-school writing assignments to distinguish knowledge mastery from AI capability.

S.t: Generative AI and Critical Coding
S.s: Teaches students to interrogate AI-generated code and algorithmic decision-making.

S.t: Generative AI and Cultural Biases
S.s: Explores how language models embed and perpetuate cultural assumptions.

S.t: Generative AI Being Used for Essay Co-writing
S.s: Examines authorship and transparency when students co-write essays with an LLM.

S.t: Understand Your LLM With the Use of a Psychology Experiment
S.s: Designs behavioral experiments letting students empirically test AI model capabilities and limitations.

S.t: Chatbot-based feedback + revision and reflection for lab reports
S.s: Students upload lab-report drafts to a custom chatbot trained on course guidelines; it gives structured feedback, they revise, then write a reflection.

S.t: Oral History Project (for "Global Game Industries")
S.s: Students interview a video-game industry professional, edit the transcript, and present the process in class.

S.t: Strengthening Your AI Fluency: Building Community Resilience
S.s: Guided use of NotebookLM and Gemini to generate and evaluate disaster-resilience planning ideas for a chosen location.

S.t: US Latino Demographics Exploration
S.s: Students use AI for basic census-data analysis to trace Latino demographic change across three geographies.

S.t: AI for Regression Analysis and More
S.s: Copilot and NotebookLM used together to build a dataset and run statistical/regression analyses.

S.t: Client Communication Strategy Pitch Deck
S.s: A business pitch-deck assignment built around client communication strategy.

S.t: Intro to AI Literacies Discussion
S.s: A structured discussion activity on the literacies required to use generative AI responsibly.

S.t: Activity: Students find conceptual errors in AI output
S.s: Instructor pre-tests ChatGPT to find a wrong answer, then has students locate and explain the conceptual error.

S.t: Employing AI to enhance in-class role playing scenarios
S.s: Students use AI to build characters and arguments ahead of in-class role-play scenarios.

S.t: Exploring cultural stereotypes via AI-generated images
S.s: Students generate AI images of cultural groups, then critique the outputs to surface embedded stereotypes.

S.t: Teaching Evolutionary Principles with AI Image Generators
S.s: Students generate a grid of AI images of one organism and use trait variation to build a phylogenetic tree.

S.t: Does AI Ask Good Questions? A Discussion Activity
S.s: Students prompt ChatGPT for discussion questions, then evaluate and improve them.

S.t: Traduttore, Traditore: A Comparative Translation Activity
S.s: Students translate a passage themselves, then compare it against ChatGPT's translation.

S.t: Incorporating GenAI into Information Literacy: Engineering Standards
S.s: Students prompt GenAI for relevant engineering standards, then must verify the responses themselves.

S.t: Using GenAI to teach information literacy in Engineering Technical Writing
S.by: Lauren Todd, Eliot Boden, Sam Lindgren
S.s: Students use GenAI to generate research keywords, then verify findings via the library's own search tools.

S.t: Piloting GPT-based asynchronous learning in a digital marketing module supporting tourism enterprises in LDCs
S.inst: Ulster Business School; Northern Regional College
S.s: A pilot project incorporated generative pretrained transformers into an asynchronous digital marketing module, exposing learners to GPT tools while focusing coursework on supporting tourism enterprises in Botswana, Uganda, and Zambia.

S.t: Cómo Entrenar tu Dragón: an ECTS-accredited GenAI literacy module for new HE lecturers
S.inst: Universidad de Las Palmas de Gran Canaria
S.s: An optional, ECTS-accredited module ('CETD23') within ULPGC's staff teacher-training programme was delivered to build new lecturers' critical AI literacy; it received a 4.84/5 satisfaction score and 100% completion of its final summative project.

S.t: Trialling ChatGPT to co-author a problem-based-learning medical case scenario
S.s: Ulster's School of Medicine trialled a single author working in dialogue with ChatGPT to draft a full PBL clinical case (a cerebral-haemorrhage headache scenario) against the school's standard template; six experienced PBL tutors then quality-checked the output and recommended further revisions before use.

S.t: Using ChatGPT as a simulated interview subject to teach critical thinking in a 'Politics of Sport' module
S.inst: School of Sport and Exercise Science, Ulster University
S.s: Undergraduate students on a 'Politics of Sport' module conducted interviews with a ChatGPT proxy playing an imagined sports figure, using questions drawn from peer-reviewed research, to practise applying academic literature to real-world scenarios and to critically probe the model's limitations.

S.t: Embedding AI tools into Mechatronics and Engineering Programming modules
S.s: Instructors on the Mechatronics 1 and Engineering Programming modules introduced AI tools (MATLAB's AI apps, generative-design software, code assistants like ChatGPT and Copilot) through structured, problem-based tutorials. Students were required to cite AI contributions in reports as they would a human collaborator, debated ethical scenarios such as an AI design relying on unauthorised proprietary data, and were taught to cross-check AI outputs against traditional engineering analysis after several submitted solutions exceeded the module's scope or reliability.

S.t: Comparing AI and student definitions to teach a language acquisition theory
S.inst: School of Communication and Media, Ulster University
S.s: In an undergraduate module on children's language acquisition, students first define and apply a core theoretical concept (e.g. syntactic bootstrapping) in groups without AI, then generate an AI definition of the same concept, then compare the two and discuss the differences. Run weekly as a flipped-classroom activity, the exercise is designed to build theoretical understanding and AI literacy at the same time.

S.t: Comparing AI-generated word embeddings and model outputs against theory
S.inst: School of Computing, Engineering and Intelligent Systems, Ulster University
S.s: In a postgraduate Deep Learning and NLP module, students manually predict word-embedding analogies and text classifications before comparing their predictions against outputs from pretrained embedding models (Word2Vec/GloVe) and transformer models (BERT/T5), analysing where and why the models diverge from theoretical expectations, including cases of bias and adversarial sensitivity.

S.t: Using AI text analysis and ChatGPT alongside close reading of Chaucer's Canterbury Tales
S.inst: School of Arts, English and Languages, Queen's University Belfast
S.s: An undergraduate module on Chaucer's Canterbury Tales at Queen's University Belfast piloted using Voyant Text Analysis, AntConc and ChatGPT to surface lexical patterns and initial thematic summaries (e.g. repeated references to 'gentillesse'), which then served as a springboard for close reading and discussion rather than a substitute for it. Seminars included debate on the ethics of using AI to hypothesise missing narrative content, such as the incomplete Cook's Tale, and documented cases where tools like AntConc misread Middle English spellings.

S.t: AI Ventures — entrepreneurship module redesign
S.s: Redesigned the "AI Ventures" entrepreneurship elective (Global Online MBA) to have students use an LLM as a "thought partner" for business plan creation, restructuring prompts to force critical evaluation of AI output.

S.t: Embedding AI into Assessment — critique-the-AI-output assignment
S.s: Students had an LLM generate a business-strategy explanation, then wrote a 250-word critique of what the output got right and wrong; the instructor reports responses "bucketed" into clear categories that enabled efficient feedback.

S.t: Exploring Generative AI in Essay Writing and Marking
S.by: Dr Margherita de Candia, with student researchers Camille Bacha De Carvalho, Duru Karadeniz and Ziti Lei
S.inst: Dept of Political Economy, King's College London
S.s: Two full-day workshops with 20 Political Science students who composed essays using the "PAIR" framework; essays were then marked by both human assessors and generative AI, with unattributed feedback given back to students, plus pre/post surveys on trust and inclusivity.

S.t: AI as tutor and critic: using tech to personalise education
S.inst: Dickson Poon School of Law, King's College London
S.s: In a Commercial Law module, students used LLM "tutor prompts" to learn about NDAs and then drafted their own, comparing drafts to precedent in class; separately, GPT-4 was given a Land Law formative question, a student answer, and marking guidance to generate feedback, which closely matched the tutor's own.

S.t: Is it worth paying for GenAI?
S.inst: Dickson Poon School of Law, King's College London
S.s: While updating a constitutional/administrative law module, systematically compared free vs paid tiers of ChatGPT, Claude and Gemini for tasks like selecting session topics and drafting workshop scenarios; found only Gemini Advanced justified its price.

S.t: AI-Augmented Formative Feedback
S.by: Dr Gary McKeown and Dr Paddy O'Connor
S.inst: School of Psychology, Queen's University Belfast
S.s: ChatGPT was used to generate feedback on student essays, which was then compared against original tutor feedback, with student perceptions of AI-feedback quality studied alongside.

S.t: AI in Business and Society — blog-writing assignment
S.by: Dr Laura Steele and Dr Bahareh Ansari
S.s: Generative AI was integrated into a blog-writing assignment on the MSc "AI in Business and Society" module; student reflective statements showed a range of engagement from idea generation to full blog drafting, capturing both benefits and challenges.

S.t: Contemporary Technologies and Accounting (ACC3043)
S.s: The module was restructured to include a guest lecture on prompt engineering plus structured discussion of AI use cases and ethics in accounting practice.

S.t: A Data Science Course Utilizing GenAI
S.by: Jonathan W. Browning, John Bustard, Neil Anderson and Leo Galway
S.inst: School of EEECS, Queen's University Belfast
S.s: ChatGPT was integrated into a postgraduate data-analysis course with a "dual-report" assessment evaluating both project outcomes and students' prompt-engineering skill; students showed enhanced analysis skills but struggled to frame effective queries.

S.t: Unit-specific AI chatbots: Reducing the search burden for students
S.by: Marios Kremantzis, Hua Jin, Anthi Chondrogianni, Aniekan Essien and Sophie Lythreatis
S.inst: Faculty of Arts, Law and Social Sciences, University of Bristol
S.s: Custom chatbots grounded in approved teaching materials were built and embedded in Blackboard Ultra for three units (about 750 students) with three matched control units; roughly 60% of use was outside working hours, assessment queries made up 43% of questions, with 81% positive feedback on deadline queries but only 49% on summative-assessment detail.

S.t: Teaching with AI in higher education: insights from practice with Blackboard Ultra
S.inst: College of Health, Science and Society, UWE Bristol
S.s: Used Blackboard Ultra's AI Conversation tool to run Socratic-questioning/role-play scenarios modelling public health workplace situations, and had students compare human peer feedback against AI-generated feedback in a "Qualitative Peer Review" assignment, including reported benefits for international students using AI-assisted translation.

S.t: 'Perfectly Frank' — using performance to demystify AI
S.by: Will Shüler, Chris Hogg and Karim Shohdy
S.inst: School of Performing and Digital Arts, Royal Holloway
S.s: A 50-minute theatrical lecture-demonstration updating Frankenstein: audience "lab assistants" were recorded and used to build an AI creature — ChatGPT (custom-prompted), ElevenLabs (voice), Midjourney (body), Runway (animation/lip-sync) — to teach AI literacy through creative practice.

S.t: Will AI revolutionise marking?
S.s: Uploaded his own assessment rubric to ChatGPT-4 and compared its grading of student work against his own marks; found initial AI over-scoring by up to 20%, persistent hallucinated details, and proposed an "AI sandwich" (human-framed task, AI first-pass feedback, human final grade) over full automation.

S.t: The LOV AI Co-Creation Approach: Creating Business Teaching Cases with Deep Research AI
S.s: A six-step "Lecturer Oversight and Verification" workflow used ChatGPT o3 Deep Research, Gemini 2.5 Pro and ElevenLabs to generate and verify a full MBA Finance & Investment teaching case, condensed into student summaries, games, chatbots and vodcasts; case development time fell from weeks to about 4 hours, and the 11-student MBA module scored 4.5/5 satisfaction with improved exam performance versus prior years.

S.t: Transparency and Disclosure of the Use of Gen-AI Co-Created Teaching Materials with MBA Students
S.s: Deployed weekly HeyGen-made summary videos, Gemini-generated formative HTML tests, and a NotebookLM chatbot in an MBA accounting/finance module, disclosing AI use to students upfront; 18 of 25 students responded to a survey rating the module 4.5/5, and students went on to use the same tools independently in other modules.

S.t: Incorporating generative AI into a module
S.s: GenAI was deliberately excluded from the statistics-learning component of an energy data-analysis course but permitted for coding help; students used it to debug error messages and discussed how GenAI might approach data analysis and stakeholder priorities, finding it "very helpful" for debugging and time-saving while the instructor stressed it can't replace statistical understanding.

S.t: Putting GenAI to work with writing rubrics
S.s: Used Microsoft Copilot (Enterprise Data Protection model) with an iterative prompt structure to draft and refine an analytic marking rubric for an undergraduate business ethics module, reducing time and tedium and easing future updates to the rubric.

S.t: Embracing AI: transforming teaching with responsible prompts
S.by: Dr Chekfoung Tan, Liam Perez and Kartik Gupta
S.inst: UCL Centre for Systems Engineering / Dept of Space and Climate Physics
S.s: Students submitted notes on business-change frameworks (e.g. Porter's Five Forces) to ChatGPT to get expanded notes and suggestions, using progressively refined prompts to explore links between organisational-change models.

S.t: Generative AI: Discussing proper use with students
S.by: Dr Vitor Zimmerer and Dr Alice Liefgreen
S.inst: Division of Psychology and Language Sciences, UCL
S.s: Delivered a 90-minute first-year tutorial, including a live student poll, where students used ChatGPT to answer questions about a pre-read article at varying Bloom's-taxonomy cognitive levels, then evaluated the AI answers themselves; feedback was largely positive.

S.t: Developing students' GenAI literacy through hackathons
S.by: Tom Gurney, with Dr Flaminia Ronca
S.inst: Dept of Targeted Intervention, Sport & Exercise Medical Sciences, UCL
S.s: A CofFEE-funded hackathon put 10 students in 5 teams through six tasks — pick a health topic, research public perceptions, compare with scientific evidence, write a report, deliver a lay talk — using ChatGPT, Google Bard, Evidence Hunt and Tome.

S.t: Using AI with literature searches
S.by: Dr Zahra Mohri and Dr Alia Galadari
S.inst: Division of Surgery & Interventional Science, UCL
S.s: A student-staff partnership on a Research Project module tested ChatGPT's ability to refine PICO research questions, generate synonyms, and build database-specific search strings across Ovid Medline, Embase and Web of Science, finding it helpful for question refinement but only partially useful for search-string translation.

S.t: Artificial intelligence and rethinking coursework assessments
S.by: Andrew Williams, with student co-creator Ellie Ingle
S.inst: BSc Applied Medical Sciences, UCL Medicine
S.s: As part of a UCL Co-Creator: AI Project, a Year 2 "Molecular Basis of Disease" coursework gave students a ChatGPT-generated grant proposal on scleroderma-associated vasculopathy and asked them to critically evaluate its scientific accuracy, rationale, experimental design and patient benefit, alongside a broader review of Year 1–2 coursework's susceptibility to ChatGPT-generated answers.

S.t: Art Meets AI
S.inst: Art History / East Asian Studies, Stanford University
S.s: In ARTHIST 254A / EASTASN 254A, students used AI image and video tools to build a virtual museum exhibition pairing human- and AI-generated artworks, presented as a "curator tour."

S.t: Decolonizing Archives: Video & AI
S.s: In ARTSTUDI 122, students used Midjourney to generate 40 images exploring AI bias in "re-imagining futures," with a documented prompt/process log and a critique session.

S.t: Music and AI
S.s: In MUSIC 356 / CS 470, progressive assignments moved from etudes to genre-classification and generative-music systems, building toward a prototyped interactive AI music application.

S.t: Media, Mind, and Black Mirror
S.s: In COMM 173A/273A, students customized a personal ChatGPT chat partner, conversed with it 2-3 times a week for a quarter, logged surveys, then wrote an auto-observational research essay using media psychology methods.

S.t: Human-Centered AI
S.by: Peter Norvig and Daniel M. Russell
S.s: In CS 139, three assignments had students write a reflective essay on AI and learning; compare designing a party invitation via AI image tools (Dall-E/Midjourney) versus a search engine; and prototype a "Wizard of Oz" human-in-the-loop system.

S.t: Technology, Innovation, and Great Power Competition
S.by: Steve Blank, Joseph Felter and Eric Volmar
S.inst: Management Science & Engineering / International Policy, Stanford University
S.s: In MS&E 296 / INTLPOL 340, the final project requires students to use AI for policy-document synthesis and to disclose and reflect on their AI use in a final presentation and 2,500-word policy memo delivered to real-world sponsors.

S.t: Equity and Governance for Artificial Intelligence
S.s: In CS 121, a group project has students red-team and evaluate AI models using methods drawn from real AI-governance policy proposals, as if advising a congressional staffer, then present their findings.

S.t: Use of Synthetic Personas in Communication Research
S.s: In a graduate seminar (COMM 372A), students run experiments with LLM-generated synthetic personas during seminar sessions to evaluate their validity as a substitute research method.

S.t: Accelerating Project Development in EECS 485 with Maizey
S.inst: Computer Science and Engineering, University of Michigan
S.s: Built a custom Maizey (U-M's RAG chatbot) assistant scoped to one course project; 40.1% of students said it saved time versus office hours or forums, 30.5% said it helped them code faster, and it scored 100% accuracy on debugging queries and over 90% on spec/conceptual queries.

S.t: Teaching U-M GPT and AI to Prepare Students for the New Era
S.inst: School of Information / CSE, University of Michigan
S.s: A dedicated course, "How to Use AI Effectively," taught students to use generative AI as a critique, idea-generation and feedback tool rather than a replacement, reporting effects on how students approached coursework such as essay feedback and budgeting.

S.t: Integrating U-M GPT for Enhanced Student Presentations in French Language Courses
S.inst: LSA Romance Languages, University of Michigan
S.s: In French 280 and 232, students used U-M GPT as a pre-presentation research and organising tool; the instructor collected survey data on student attitudes, including concerns about accuracy, ethics and over-reliance.

S.t: Utilizing Maizey to Enhance Exam Preparation in SQL and Databases Course
S.inst: School of Information, University of Michigan
S.s: Built a Maizey bot for SI 564 that answers common exam-prep questions around the clock, freeing office hours for harder questions.

S.t: Enhancing Coding Proficiency with Code Tailor
S.inst: School of Information / Engineering, University of Michigan
S.s: Built "Code Tailor," a GPT-based system that iteratively refines feedback and prompts on students' incorrect code and generates personalised mixed-up-code puzzles after repeated failures.

S.t: Enhancing Grading Quality with GraderGPT in Large Courses
S.inst: School of Kinesiology, University of Michigan
S.s: Built GraderGPT using real Canvas submissions, rubrics and grader notes to auto-score writing assignments as a check against human grader variance, piloted in two courses with preliminary positive feedback.

S.t: Virtual Mock Patient in Doctorate of Physical Therapy Program at U-M Flint
S.by: UM-Flint Doctor of Physical Therapy Program & UM-Ann Arbor Kinesiology
S.s: An AI-simulated patient was integrated into the curriculum so first-year Doctor of Physical Therapy students can repeatedly practise communication skills without relying solely on standardized-patient actors.

S.t: Doctoring Abdominal Case
S.s: A virtual ER patient simulation (a nausea/vomiting case) with guardrails preventing the AI from giving diagnoses is used for history-taking and physical-exam practice.

S.t: Gender Informed Health Care
S.s: A virtual-patient exercise lets students repeatedly practise gender-affirming, trauma-informed history-taking and full case presentation to build confidence.

S.t: SciTrunk – Navigating U-M Medical School Year 1
S.s: About 170 first-year medical students use Maizey to query 500+ files of lecture slides and transcripts and generate practice questions that guide rather than give away their reasoning.

S.t: AI Literacy Instruction in First-Year Writing Courses
S.inst: John S. Knight Institute for Writing, Cornell University
S.s: In WRIT 1370/80, ran parallel "AI-free zone" and "generative AI integration" assignment tracks (brainstorming, outlining, summarizing with ChatGPT), each with required student reflections.

S.t: Building AI Literacy through Visualizing Innovative Textile Projects
S.inst: Fiber Science & Apparel Design, Cornell University
S.s: In FSAD 4660 (19 seniors), students used Midjourney weekly to generate and refine design images, culminating in a poster recreating past semesters' physical designs using AI.

S.t: Color-coding Strategy to Improve Student Written Argumentation
S.inst: Public and Ecosystem Health, Vet College, Cornell University
S.s: In VTPEH 6107 (50 MPH students), a color-coded claim/evidence-marking technique was used across three assignments specifically as a response to AI-written essays lacking argument structure.

S.t: Exploring AI Image Generation for Landscape Architecture Designs
S.s: In LARCH 5060 (20 graduate students), students generated 80 AI images from varied prompts using DALL-E2 and Midjourney, critiqued the outputs for bias and limitations, then hand-built a 3D model from a chosen AI image.

S.t: Writing about Controversies in American Politics: Using AI to Examine Counterarguments and Definitions
S.s: In GOVT 2817 (73 students), students first argued a position on a political controversy independently, then prompted ChatGPT to do the same task and compared the outputs for specificity and quality.

S.t: Integrating Generative AI in Quantitative Courses
S.inst: Scheller College of Business, Georgia Tech
S.s: In Management Statistics (255 students), students built personalized GPT agents paired with Excel for business problems, with weekly Excel-vs-ChatGPT homework, a case-based AI mini-capstone, and a guest lecture from PwC on GenAI in practice.

S.t: Integrating AI Literacy into GT Writing and Communication Courses
S.s: Piloted four course redesigns and faculty workshops across 1,637 students and 22 faculty on critically engaging with ChatGPT in academic writing.

S.t: Integrating AI-Driven Collaborative Learning Tools in High Enrollment CS Courses
S.by: Nimisha Roy and Rodrigo Borela Valente
S.s: Built a GenAI-powered learning platform for CS 1301 (970 students) giving error identification, tailored feedback, and problem recommendations via deep knowledge tracing.

S.t: Portrait of Practice: Generative AI in Writing 201
S.s: Added a dedicated AI unit to Writing 201 (History of Writing Studies) and hosted Duke's Vice Provost for Learning Innovation as a guest speaker; students then chose AI as the topic for their capstone Sustained Inquiry Research projects, with three student projects documented.

S.t: Debating Dead Philosophers with Generative AI
S.inst: School for Historical, Philosophical and Religious Studies, Arizona State University
S.s: In Introduction to Philosophy of Science, students role-play a philosopher (e.g. Karl Popper) and use ChatGPT to debate an AI-generated rival (e.g. Rudolf Carnap) across three assignments; scores improved from the first to second assignment, engagement was higher (voluntary repeat use), and final-exam performance improved.

S.t: 'Charlie' AI Essay Feedback Tool
S.s: A custom AI tool, "Charlie," gives instant rubric-based feedback on drafts so students can revise before the deadline, used across her classes over roughly six years and recognized in EDUCAUSE's 2023 Horizons Report.

S.t: ChatLTV: AI Faculty Co-Pilot
S.s: Built a RAG chatbot (GPT-4 + Pinecone) trained on 200 course documents (about 15M words) for the MBA "Launching Tech Ventures" course (about 250 students across 3 sections), embedded in Slack; 170+ students sent 3,000+ queries, about 40% rated it 4-5/5, and 99% used it privately.

S.t: 'I Secretly Let ChatGPT Take My Final Exam'
S.s: In an Algorithms course, ran weekly in-class demos comparing ChatGPT's answers to students' on algorithm questions, then secretly submitted ChatGPT's final-exam answers under a fake name for blind grading; every student in the morning section outscored it (a C-minus, 72.5), while the afternoon section's AI score fell in the bottom third.

S.t: GenAI as an 'assisted format' for research rationales
S.by: Gee Chong Ling and A/Prof Jai Tree
S.inst: School of Biotechnology and Biomolecular Science, UNSW
S.s: In MICR2011 (Microbiology 1), students were permitted to use GenAI as an "assisted format" for developing lab research rationales; in Week 1 the instructors showed students an AI-generated response with fabricated references to demonstrate its limitations, and required screenshotted reference lists to verify authentic sourcing.

S.t: Critiquing ChatGPT output to build communication skills
S.s: In PSYC2061, students used ChatGPT to draft a 500-word media release on published research, then critiqued it against a marking rubric, produced two further drafts without AI, and finished with a recorded video abstract.

S.t: Simulated NSC role-play to minimise GenAI reliance
S.s: In ARTS3783, essay assessment was replaced with a foreign-policy review plus a simulated National Security Council role-play; GenAI produced "generic, incoherent" outputs unsuited to the live-negotiation format, reportedly increasing engagement.

S.t: Process-based group assessment with AI-assisted brainstorming
S.by: A/Prof Zixiu Guo and A/Prof Carmen Leong
S.s: In INFS5848, the group assignment was redesigned from a final report into an iterative process with weekly check-ins; students could use ChatGPT for brainstorming project ideas but had to personalise and justify them.

S.t: Standardising project-assessment design with ChatGPT
S.inst: School of Mechanical and Mechatronic Engineering, UTS
S.s: Used ChatGPT to standardise project-assessment design across academics in the School of Mechanical and Mechatronic Engineering.

S.t: Redesigning written assessments into visual-artefact assessments
S.s: Tested ChatGPT against original written assessment tasks, then redesigned them into visual-artefact assessments.

S.t: AI-related legal and technology issues assessment
S.s: Built an assessment asking students to respond to new AI-related legal and technology issues.

S.t: Comparing ChatGPT output against expert material
S.inst: School of Sport, Exercise and Rehabilitation, UTS
S.s: Had students compare and contrast ChatGPT output against expert material in the field.

S.t: 'Don't be sorry, just declare it' — GenAI disclosure appendix
S.s: Ran a disclosure-based system requiring a mandatory "GenAI appendix" in essays across two consecutive course offerings (214 combined students), combined with evidence retention and partial in-person assessment; reports students "responded well."

S.t: 'Virtual Peer' AI chatbot pilot
S.inst: School of Psychological Sciences, Macquarie University
S.s: A RAG-based chatbot built on Azure OpenAI/Copilot Studio was piloted in a second-year psychology unit with 1,400 students; students using it scored 9.45% higher on average, sent 20,800+ messages (80% after-hours), with an NPS of 92.

S.t: NotebookLM + RISE framework for responsible AI critique
S.inst: Faculty of Business and Economics, University of Auckland
S.s: In BUS202 (1,000+ students per semester), loaded NotebookLM with deliberately contradictory sources on AI-driven brand personas and had students run structured RISE prompts, iteratively critiquing and refining the AI's output, moving students from passive acceptance to evidence-based critique.

S.t: Interactive oral assessment vs. AI-suspected lab reports
S.inst: Chemical and Materials Engineering, University of Auckland
S.s: After suspecting AI-written lab reports in CHEMMAT 206, replaced one written report with a 10-minute oral interview where students critique an AI-generated paragraph built from a NotebookLM trained on the lab procedure, with grades and feedback recorded for the following semester.

S.t: Cogniti AI agent as Socratic tutor for large first-year biology cohort
S.inst: School of Biological Sciences, University of Auckland
S.s: Embedded a steered Cogniti AI agent, configured as a first-year cell biology lecturer persona, into Canvas for 24/7 Socratic support in BIOSCI 107 (1,600+ students), reporting over 60% engagement — double the participation of prior discussion boards — and real-time insight into student struggles via anonymised logs.

S.t: Cogniti pilot across first-year courses
S.by: Dr Marion Blumenstein, Dr Andrea Kolb, Dr Bettina Schwenger and Prof Jason Stephens
S.inst: Science, Engineering & Design, Arts & Education, University of Auckland
S.s: A cross-faculty team piloted Cogniti (built by the University of Sydney) as course-admin and Socratic-tutor agents in 3 courses in Semester 1 2025, expanding to 18 courses across all faculties in Semester 2, running SRL surveys (400-530 responses per semester) and focus groups that found it supported deeper learning and task completion for many students.

S.t: GenAI use case in Data Structures / Numerical Optimization
S.s: In COSC 222 and COSC 406, had AI tools generate solutions for students to critique alongside peer solutions, used AI to double-check instructor-written solutions, and generated MCQ variants; found it useful for efficiency but flagged AI math errors requiring verification.

S.t: Generative AI chatbots for large biology courses
S.inst: Cell & Systems Biology, University of Toronto
S.s: Built custom chatbots "ChatBIO130"/"ChatBIO230" for BIO130H1/BIO230H1, giving 24/7 practice Q&A integrated with lecture and lab content, reporting reduced student anxiety and heavy pre-exam use.

S.t: Copilot-generated clinical case studies
S.s: Used a 7-step prompting process with Microsoft Copilot to build bilingual child-language clinical cases for formative and summative use in SLP courses, with strong positive student feedback.

S.t: LLMs for real-world global affairs tasks
S.inst: Munk School of Global Affairs, University of Toronto
S.s: In "Real Time Data and AI for Global Affairs Intelligence," students used ChatGPT, Gemini and DeepSeek on self-chosen policy problems, compared model outputs, and ran adversarial tests on them.

S.t: 'Beat the Machines' recruitment assignment
S.inst: Industrial Relations & Human Resources, University of Toronto
S.s: In IRE348, students used AI for job and organisational analysis, then had to critique and out-write AI-drafted job advertisements.

S.t: AI in financial data analysis essays
S.inst: Rotman School of Management, University of Toronto
S.s: In RSM230, ran two contrasting essay conditions — AI-drafted-then-revised versus self-written-then-AI-edited — on news and equity-price analysis.

S.t: Literature review with Research Rabbit + ChatGPT
S.s: In ECO225, students built visual citation maps in Research Rabbit and AI-assisted summary tables in ChatGPT, with required source verification.

S.t: Critical comparison of student vs. Copilot literature reviews
S.inst: Faculty of Dentistry, University of Toronto
S.s: In HMB474H1F, a three-part assignment compared a student-written literature review to Copilot-generated reviews, with a guest session from a Google executive on effective AI use.

S.t: AI-assisted physiology lab analysis
S.inst: Kinesiology & Physical Education, University of Toronto
S.s: In KPE360, students collected real physiological data, then used ChatGPT and Copilot to generate an analysis response they had to critically evaluate against the published literature.

S.t: Graphic Novel
S.inst: School of Management & Marketing, Curtin University
S.s: Postgraduate HR Development students select and use a GenAI tool of their choice to generate imagery for a training manual on HR themes, graded on storytelling and content rather than image quality.

S.t: Theoretical Audit
S.inst: School of Management & Marketing, Curtin University
S.s: Business Information Systems students conduct a simulated security audit of a fictional AI company, interacting with AI chatbot "employees" and using an LLM of their choice as a "junior intern" whose outputs they must validate.

S.t: Avatar Simulation with AI Feedback
S.inst: School of Allied Health (Physiotherapy), Curtin University
S.s: Physiotherapy students practise clinical communication with "Michael," an AI-powered patient avatar giving immediate conversational feedback, combined with staff and peer feedback.

S.t: Critical AI Comparison Report
S.inst: Faculty of Business & Law, Curtin University
S.s: In ECOM1000, students first analyse investment portfolios manually using Modern Portfolio Theory and the Sharpe Ratio, then re-run the same analysis with AI tools, then critically compare the two, documenting prompts and outputs throughout.

S.t: Integrating Generative AI into Live Case Studies for Experiential Learning in Operations Management
S.by: David Ernesto Salinas-Navarro, Eliseo Vilalta-Perdomo, Jaime Alberto Palma-Mendoza and Martina Carlos-Arroyo
S.inst: Aston Business School, Aston University (course institution)
S.s: A UK postgraduate operations management module ran a live case study with a real local ethnic restaurant, using GenAI as a scaffolding "cognitive partner" through Kolb's experiential learning cycle; thematic analysis of module outputs and feedback surveys showed engagement and critical decision-making, though outcomes varied with students' GenAI literacy.

S.t: Just ask Mattie: Supporting first-year business maths students with a custom-built AI tutor
S.by: Malgorzata Korolkiewicz, Wolfgang Mayer and Nicholas Fewster-Young
S.s: Built and deployed "Mattie.AI," a RAG-based Socratic-style chatbot tutor, for a first-year business mathematics course aimed at students reluctant to ask for help publicly; evaluated via interaction logs and student surveys, finding it a promising supplement that needs more scaffolding for effective use.

S.t: Building capacity for AI-powered learning: A collaborative trial of conversational agents in higher education
S.s: 26 university staff built and tested conversational learning agents on the Cogniti platform over six months in 2024, producing 20 agents across disciplines, 9 of which were deployed into actual teaching, with unanimous usability satisfaction and 95% willingness to recommend.

S.t: The AI prescription: Educating future doctors for a tech-driven clinic
S.s: Ran a half-day residential-school AI activity for pre-medical students, combining hands-on AI exploration, scenario-based learning and design-thinking ideation, with follow-up focus groups showing improved understanding of AI's capabilities and limits.

S.t: Smart scaffolds, smarter learners? Analysing the cognitive outcomes of AR and chatbot feedback
S.by: Jerry Chih-Yuan Sun, Even Yi-Wen Liu and Jocelyn Ting-Yu Shen
S.inst: National Yang Ming Chiao Tung University
S.s: A randomised experiment with 118 university students in a scientific-simulation inquiry task compared traditional, chatbot-only, AR-only, and combined chatbot+AR feedback, finding chatbot scaffolding significantly increased cognitive and intrinsic load, and combined chatbot-AR increased extraneous load compared to AR alone.

S.t: Enhancing self-regulated learning with large language models: A pilot study on the feasibility of local deployment
S.s: Co-designed a self-regulated-learning chatbot with 61 students, then ran a two-week field trial with 7 participants comparing a locally-deployed (offline/private) LLM against a cloud version; the chatbot supported goal-setting and reflection, with users trusting the local version more despite slower responses.

S.t: Design and evaluation of an LLM literature review assistant
S.s: Built a RAG-based web tool giving honours and postgraduate students rubric-aligned LLM feedback on draft literature reviews; a 10-participant user study showed measurable writing improvement and positive feedback on citation and structure guidance.

S.t: 'Alex' — GenAI use in a HASS subject
S.by: Josephine Hook, Andrew Junor, Cathy Sell and Carmen Sapsed
S.s: In one HASS subject, a class activity had students prompt a GenAI tool together, then gave explicit permission to use AI on a final independent research project; none of 80 students chose to use it, and the case concluded GenAI was a "distraction" for that discipline.

S.t: 'Brodie' — optional AI-assisted essay task in cultural studies
S.by: Josephine Hook, Andrew Junor, Cathy Sell and Carmen Sapsed
S.s: In a cultural studies subject, an optional alternative essay task let students ask GenAI to draft an essay, then critique and edit it with a tracked-changes and prompt-transcript submission; 20% of students opted in, reporting higher satisfaction and, on average, higher-quality work than the conventional-essay group.

S.t: 'Cal' — ATLAS policy-simulation platform
S.by: Josephine Hook, Andrew Junor, Cathy Sell and Carmen Sapsed
S.s: A cross-faculty team built "ATLAS," a custom LLM-based platform simulating a live social-media environment, for a role-play during a public-health-crisis policy-simulation unit; students who engaged received higher grades on average and reported high engagement.

S.t: 'Dana' — AI recruiter feedback on job applications
S.by: Josephine Hook, Andrew Junor, Cathy Sell and Carmen Sapsed
S.s: Used the same ATLAS platform to build an AI "recruiter" giving students in a creative-discipline career-development unit structured feedback on job and grant applications against real industry standards, freeing the human lecturer to give higher-level specialist feedback.

S.t: Challenges and opportunities in using ChatGPT as a team member to promote code review education
S.by: Paula de Barba and Chakkrit (Kla) Tantithamthavorn
S.s: Novice and experienced programmers used ChatGPT as a "team member" during code-review exercises; novices reported deeper understanding and experienced students engaged in iterative prompt optimisation, though researchers found mixed results, with some concerns for students lacking foundational knowledge.

S.t: Exploring the integration of Generative AI in assessment in a tertiary context
S.s: Pre-service language-teacher postgraduate students used GenAI tools to plan and generate ideas for an academic essay assignment; thematic analysis of student reflections and surveys identified benefits for idea generation alongside risks from inaccuracy and bias.

S.t: Interactive Oral Assessments replacing written exams — zero academic integrity breaches since 2022
S.by: Dr Chris Della Vedova and Dr Sarah Davey
S.s: Replaced the final written, invigilated exam in Laboratory Medicine and Biomedical Science courses with a 20-minute one-on-one Interactive Oral Assessment (IOA): a live conversation with unseen follow-up questions drawn from a pool, probing depth of understanding in real time — a pre-generated AI answer can't survive live follow-up questioning.
S.ev: Zero academic integrity breaches recorded since IOAs were introduced in 2022, versus a nonzero rate under the prior written-exam format; also a reported 15% rise in final assessment scores and 20% rise in overall course performance. Reported via a university press release/instructor interview rather than a peer-reviewed breach-count table, so treat the exact size of the effect with some caution.
S.q: Since implementing the IOAs in 2022, we've not had a single academic integrity breach.

S.t: Three-year, 25-subject rollout of Interactive Oral Assessment reduces academic dishonesty
S.by: M. Droulers, A. Krautloher, S. Shaeri
S.s: An institution-wide, three-year project rolled out Interactive Oral Assessment across 25 subjects as a response to GenAI-enabled misconduct risk, surveying 290 students on their experience and comparing oral assessment against written work on validity, reliability and student preference.
S.ev: Reports that IOAs "reduce academic dishonesty" across the 25-subject sample, alongside higher student confidence and preference for oral over written assessment. The paper is paywalled beyond the abstract, so it's unclear whether the dishonesty figure comes from actual case counts or from self-report/perception items — worth confirming directly before treating this as strong evidence.

S.t: AI Assessment Scale institution-wide pilot cuts GenAI misconduct cases while raising attainment
S.by: Mike Perkins, Leon Furze, Jasper Roe, Jason MacVaugh
S.s: Rather than a blanket ban, roughly 2,500 students across the institution were given a 5-level AI Assessment Scale (No AI → Full AI) specifying exactly how much GenAI use was permitted per assessment, paired with redesigned rubrics, comparing the 2023 and 2024 academic years.
S.ev: Reports "a significant reduction in academic misconduct cases related to GenAI" institution-wide from 2023 to 2024, alongside a 5.9% rise in student attainment and a 33.3% rise in module pass rates over the same period. The exact misconduct case-count/percentage wasn't extractable from the accessible text — only the qualitative "significant reduction" framing — so the headline misconduct figure is less precise than the attainment numbers.
S.q: Banning or blocking GenAI tools has proven ineffective.

S.t: Automatic speech recognition for transcribing child-language-acquisition recordings
S.inst: School of Communication and Media, Ulster University
S.s: Automatic speech recognition (local ASR models, including Batchalign) transcribes recordings of children's speech collected 2013–2020, replacing manual transcription at the data-processing stage of corpus-based linguistic research into language acquisition.

S.t: NLP-based harmonization of psychology survey items across independent studies
S.s: An NLP tool (Harmony, built on an SBERT transformer model) identifies semantically similar questionnaire items across independently run surveys, enabling researchers to pool and jointly analyse otherwise incompatible psychology/public-health datasets — used at the data-preparation stage of the research pipeline.

S.t: LLM-driven avatars as a research instrument for conflict-resolution training studies
S.inst: School of Communication and Media, Ulster University
S.s: An LLM engine (Convai) drives 3D non-player-character avatars that simulate conflict scenarios, used as the actual research/data-collection instrument for studying and training practitioners' conflict-resolution skills.
